excerpt_en: Wednesday. I spent the morning just getting work done, and in the evening, I went to a Japanese meetup Halloween event. That said, since most of the people there are Japanese, not many were in costume. A few of the non-Japanese folks
body_en:
Wednesday.

I spent the morning just getting work done,

and in the evening, I went to a Japanese meetup

Halloween event.

That said,

since most of the people there are Japanese,

not many were in costume.

A few of the non-Japanese folks were dressed up though.

I didn't have a costume with me,

but I did have my neck-hanging masu,

so I wore that and joined in!

It was a Halloween event,

but for me,

it was no different from a regular Japanese meetup,

so my goal was simply

to increase the number of people who know me.

So I went around talking to all sorts of people,

and people came up to talk to me too.

I think there were probably about 100 people there.

That said,

it's not like I can talk to everyone,

and there's also the matter of

whether I actually want to talk to someone - it's a "feelings thing," haha.

Normally I don't really approach people myself,

but I tried talking to some university students from Japan,

and honestly, it was pretty boring.

Not all of them were boring,

but the ones who came through university programs

don't have a strong desire to learn something here, so their energy is low.

When that happens, you can't have a real back-and-forth,

and communication becomes really difficult.

There's also the age gap, I suppose.

Some university students are genuinely curious,

and since we're both doing unusual things,

we can find each other interesting,

but when that's not the case, it's tough.

Well, looking back at myself,

when I was a first-year university student on my first trip abroad,

I was only thinking about

"doing a homestay and learning about foreign cultures,"

so I guess it's about the same.

Speaking from my own experience

and from watching the university students around me,

I've come to realize that

the environment you put yourself in

really changes things a lot.

Yeah, at networking events like this,

approaching Japanese university students on my own

just wasn't a good fit for me.

After all,

people who are interested in me or my activities

will find information about me somewhere

and come talk to me,

so I should focus my energy on that side.

So the people I should be approaching

aren't Japanese people, but people from other countries.

Once again,

it was a great opportunity to think about these things!

excerpt_en: Well, it's the last day of October! That means it's Halloween! This is my first time being abroad during this season, and what's more, Halloween actually originated in Ireland! That's exactly why I came back to Ireland just for this time,
body_en:
Well, it's the last day of October!

That means it's Halloween!

This is my first time being abroad

during this season,

and what's more,

Halloween actually originated in Ireland!

That's exactly why I came back

to Ireland just for this time of year.

So with that,

I knocked out work efficiently in the morning,

and headed out for Halloween in the evening!

The other day, I found info about a Halloween parade,

and it was supposed to be held on a famous street,

so I went to check it out.

Already,

tons of people were waiting along the sides of the road for it to start.

The event was supposed to start at 7 PM,

so I headed to the main street around 6:40.

Alright, 7 o'clock.

Nothing's happening yet.

Unlike Japan,

this country isn't strict about time,

so I figured I'd just wait a bit.

10 minutes, 20 minutes, 30 minutes passed.

It seemed like it was almost time

for the street to become pedestrian-only,

but after about 50 minutes, a security guard came over and said:

"There's no parade today!"

Huh???

What do you mean??

Can you believe it -

a massive crowd had gathered to see the parade,

and there was no parade at all!! Haha

I thought I'd get to see an awesome parade

in Ireland, the birthplace of Halloween,

but there was nothing!! Haha

There was even a website for it,

and yet no event - I couldn't help but laugh!

As a postscript,

it turned out this was fake news.

A massive number of people

had gathered to see the parade,

which confirmed that the demand for a Halloween parade is incredibly high,

and I got the impression that there were

especially a lot of people from India, Hong Kong, and/or China.

Still,

I can't believe I got fooled by fake news! Haha

Since Halloween originated in Ireland,

my expectations were high that there'd be a big parade, so it was a bit disappointing.

But when it comes to events,

Japan's cosplay-focused Halloween

and America's Halloween

are clearly more developed

as festivals, I'd say!

Looking at it from an event organizer's perspective,

countries that promote things well

as a business

naturally generate more excitement,

and I learned that just because a place

is the origin of something

doesn't mean it's going to be amazing.

These kinds of learnings and stories

only come from actually going there in person,

and there are good things and bad things,

but it reminds me once again that I'm living quite an unusual life.

On to Chapter 2!

This article is about October 31st,

so with this,

Chapter 1 of my Ireland Activity Log

comes to an end.

Articles from November 1st onward

will be in "Ireland Activity Log Part 2"!

If you find this spontaneous,

go-with-the-flow account of my activities

in Europe entertaining,

I'd be really happy if you'd subscribe

to Chapter 2 of the activity log as well!

From here,

FOMUS will spend the month of November

gradually building

a subscription community.

First, we're aiming for 50 members.

Basically, I plan to reach out mainly to people

I've met over the past three and a half years

and who I've had the pleasure of working with through FOMUS.

Since it's a subscription,

money is involved,

so I'll be putting even more effort into my activities than ever,

and I'd be happy if you could enjoy the story of spreading the FOMUS brand to the world.

And I'd be thrilled if you could

help build up FOMUS together with me!

I'll be stepping on the gas even harder in Chapter 2!

Thanks for your continued support!!

title_en: [Special Edition] Going to a Hair Salon in Dublin
excerpt_en: It's the last day of August, so time for a look back at this past month! ...is what I'd like to say, but I've been writing bits and pieces along the way, so I'll skip that. Normally I write one article per day, but today I had so much input that
body_en:
It's the last day of August,

so time for a look back at this past month!

...is what I'd like to say,

but I've been writing bits and pieces along the way, so I'll skip that.

Normally I write one article per day,

but today I had so much input

that the output will be more than usual too.

*Just a reminder: this activity log is

a place for MaSU of FOMUS to document the things I've learned and experienced while expanding the FOMUS brand in Ireland from scratch with zero connections, and interacting with local people.

But honestly, keeping all of this just to myself

wouldn't be very fun,

so I'm sharing my output with subscribers like this, in hopes that it might be even a little bit helpful.

Anyway, on to the main topic.

Going to my first hair salon in Ireland

I left Japan on June 21st,

traveled through America and Spain for about 5 weeks,

arrived in Ireland, and a month has passed.

I hadn't gotten a haircut in about 2 months.

Honestly, I was dying to get it cut, but I decided to wait until this timing.

The place I went to was

a hair salon called "Maru,"

which is the first Japanese-owned

hair salon in Ireland.

They also have many Japanese hairstylists on staff, so it's an incredibly reassuring place for Japanese people.

Of course, that also means the prices are higher.

Why did I choose this salon?

And why this timing?

There are several reasons.

1. I wanted to get a cut before the Medoc Marathon

2. To build connections in Dublin

3. To spread the FOMUS brand

First, the Medoc Marathon is coming up next week. It's in France, so the temperature will be higher than in Ireland.

And there will be lots of opportunities to take photos with people.

So I wanted to time my haircut for that.

The second reason is

"to build connections in Dublin."

You might wonder - a hair salon for making connections?

But the owner has put down roots here and runs the shop in this area.

I could choose the salon

and pick the stylist,

and for me, the owner Reina was the only choice.

Of course, she's also the most expensive.

*110 euros (about 18,000 yen)

But if I went with Reina,

the time spent cutting my hair

would be 100% mine to use.

In other words, I could ask her whatever I wanted.

Compared to me, who's only been here for a month,

she knows this area incomparably better,

and there's no doubt her network is extensive.

From my strategic side's perspective,

she's one of the key Japanese figures living in Dublin.

There are several Japanese communities in Dublin,

but there aren't many people

who have lived here for a long time (about 10 years).

That's exactly why,

if word gets out that "MaSU, who recently came to Ireland, is an interesting guy," the established figures around her won't ignore it.

I've been gathering information over this past month,

so I know all the Japanese-owned shops in Ireland. Going to them and giving first is one way to enter the "village,"

in my view.

That ties into the third reason -

spreading the FOMUS brand.

By connecting with key people,

if they find "MaSU" interesting,

there's a chance that my "content" will get some attention too.

Content doesn't come first -

the most important thing is getting people to find "MaSU" himself interesting,

and my mission on this first visit was to make sure Reina

thought at least a little bit, "What an interesting guy."

*The longer my hair, the more

cutting time there is, and the more time I can monopolize

So I researched everything I could about Reina

before going.

By researching the other person beforehand,

it conveys to them that "this person is genuinely interested in me."

She didn't seem to have personal social media,

so I gathered information by Googling and such.

I also needed to talk about myself,

so I shared my activities up to now and what I'm doing in Dublin.

Just the day before,

I had gone to the Japanese restaurant "Daruma"

and got them to display a masu,

and earlier today I had organized

an international exchange event,

so I think I left the impression of

"a guy who takes on any challenge"

and "someone who's already met other key figures and earned some recognition."

So anyway,

what ended up happening in the end...

https://www.notion.so

I successfully got them to display

a FOMUS masu in the salon!!

That makes the second shop in Ireland, right after yesterday!!

Reina herself offered,

saying I could display the masu!

I gratefully took her up on the offer,

handed over a masu,

and got it placed at the "checkout" area

that every customer passes through,

as a "candy holder."

This is truly something to be grateful for,

and I feel like I really need to do more promotion too.

Honestly, if I'd gone with a stylist other than Reina,

the price would have been about 30 euros (around 5,000 yen) cheaper.

But if I'd been stingy about that,

this opportunity would never have happened.

After all, talking directly to the person who has decision-making power

is overwhelmingly faster,

and everything gets decided on the spot.

I invested in this "connection."

Over the past few years, as an address hopper,

I've gone to all sorts of places

and met all kinds of people,

and talking to the top person is always the quickest,

and if the top person says OK, everything changes.

I was able to make this judgment

because of this kind of experience and knowledge.

So anyway,

that's the story of how I went to a hair salon and got them to display a FOMUS masu on the spot.

They also had a sales section,

but rather than selling masu with just the FOMUS logo,

I thought it would be great if in the future

I could include the "Maru" logo too

and offer them as a collaboration.

That'll depend on how hard I work going forward.

So yeah,

today had so many learnings

that I wrote 2 articles.

Tomorrow starts September!

And right off the bat, I have a walking event and a card game event lined up.

I'm going to make sure they're a blast,

get people to find MaSU entertaining,

and work hard to get them interested in FOMUS too!

https://www.notion.so

excerpt_en: Hey there, I'm MaSU. I run the masu brand "FOMUS" and work as a photographer, traveling all over Japan. After finishing 3 months of activity in the Philippines from February to April 2024, I then traveled across Japan for about 6 weeks,
body_en:
Hey there, I'm MaSU.

I run the masu brand "FOMUS"

and work as a photographer,

traveling all over Japan.

After finishing 3 months of activity

in the Philippines from February to April 2024,

I then traveled across Japan for about 6 weeks,

and now I'm heading into the overseas battle!

As a prologue,

let me share just a bit about my activities in Japan.

May

What You Gain from Traveling | MaSU [FOMUS]

June

The Overseas Battle Is About to Begin! | MaSU [FOMUS]

I ran a crowdfunding campaign,

started an art gallery, and

took on various challenges

even during my time in Japan.

For those of you who are too busy to read the articles above,

here's a quick summary:

Through the crowdfunding campaign for FOMUS's global challenge

with the masu brand "FOMUS,"

I was able to raise about 1.5 million yen.

On top of that,

with FOMUS's funds

and support from people who invest in MaSU

outside of the crowdfunding, I'll be taking on challenges overseas for about a year this time.

And on Sagi Island in Hiroshima Prefecture,

we're creating FOMUS's first art gallery.

Together with FOMUS community members,

people from the Sagi Island area,

Sento-kun, who will co-manage it with me,

and his friends,

we're going to create

a completely new kind of creative gallery

together!!

Currently, FOMUS has multiple projects running simultaneously.

Over the past 3 years, I've been

planting many seeds domestically as well.

For that purpose, I gave up having a home

as an address hopper and went to all sorts of places.

That experience has shaped who I am today.

What happens when you add overseas experience to that?

That's something I'm looking forward to myself.

And the stronger I become,

the bigger the FOMUS brand grows,

the more people join us,

and the more possibilities will expand.

At FOMUS, we create new entertainment and creative works

centered on the traditional Japanese craft of "masu,"

together with community members around the world,

generating new value.

Well then, first I'm off to America.

excerpt_en: June 21, 2024. Hey there, I'm MaSU. I run the masu brand "FOMUS" and work as a photographer, traveling all over Japan. To Los Angeles. Today I arrived in Los Angeles!
body_en:
June 21, 2024

Hey there, I'm MaSU.

I run the masu brand "FOMUS"

and work as a photographer,

traveling all over Japan.

To Los Angeles

Today I arrived in Los Angeles!

From the airport, I had pre-purchased a ticket for the bus into the city (FlyawayBus) and was able to board without any issues.

I had booked just one night's accommodation

in Hollywood for today,

so to drop off my luggage,

I took the subway straight to the Hollywood station.

Los Angeles has a lot of subway lines,

so I bought a TapCard (like a PASMO) at the station.

As expected,

I had a headache and drowsiness from jet lag,

and when I looked up whether I should sleep, apparently you shouldn't, so after dropping off my luggage at the accommodation, I went exploring around Hollywood.

By the way, for jet lag,

apparently getting sunlight

helps adjust your body clock.

Despite it being a weekday,

tons of people were walking around the streets.

I barely saw any Japanese people.

And for good reason.

For lunch, I wanted to save money, so I went to Subway, but a drink and a sandwich came to $13.

At $1 = 159 yen,

that's over 2,000 yen!

2,000 yen for this amount of food.

In Japan, this wouldn't even cost 1,000 yen.

The impact of the weak yen is that strong,

and I really felt I needed to think carefully

about how to save money.

I had no plans for today,

so I just casually explored.

That said, the main part of Hollywood isn't that big, so you can see everything pretty quickly.

I found a free chair at a spot that's both a shopping mall and a viewing point for the Hollywood sign, and sat down, but the drowsiness was so bad that I passed out a few times. Haha

That's how sleepy I was.

After checking in around 4 PM,

I did some laptop work,

and by 6 PM, I was looking for dinner.

Honestly, everything was too expensive,

so I decided to try a convenience store.

Frankly, even a convenience store salad costs over 1,000 yen, and knowing Japanese prices, I thought "No way am I spending 1,000 yen on this!" - so I bought a relatively cheap muffin.

Saving money is important, but considering my health, I can't be too stingy about it,

so I got the feeling that when staying long-term, I should cook my own pasta and such.

Then, nighttime.

I wanted to see what Hollywood is like at night,

so I went out

for a stroll.

At night, it had that Hollywood vibe.

So that's how I spent the day - exploring from morning to night

and working at the accommodation.

I considered going to bars

to spread the FOMUS brand,

but since I knew I'd end up drinking

starting tomorrow anyway, I decided to skip it today. Haha

And with that,

my activities in Los Angeles have begun!

I'll be making new connections and interactions in full swing!

Check out the YouTube here

https://youtu.be/hhT9BjIlv-k

excerpt_en: June 22nd. Alright, starting today, I've reunited with GPS runner Shimizu-san, who I ran around with for about a month during the Taiwan circumnavigation project starting May 2023! Shimizu-san finished his activities in Peru and was stopping by Los Angeles before heading back to Japan,
body_en:
June 22nd

Alright, starting today,

I've reunited with GPS runner Shimizu-san,

who I ran around with for about a month during

the Taiwan circumnavigation project starting May 2023!

Shimizu-san finished his activities in Peru

and was stopping by Los Angeles

before heading back to Japan,

so I'd actually timed

my arrival in LA to match his schedule.

Our meeting spot was, right off the bat, Dodger Stadium,

and we started by watching a game with Shohei Ohtani playing!

Ohtani hit a home run that day,

but unfortunately, we didn't make it in time for that moment. Haha

When it was Ohtani's turn at bat,

the cheers from the crowd got louder,

and seeing a Japanese person excelling on the world stage

up close like that

made me even more determined to do my best too.

#TakingTheFOMUSBrandToTheWorld

After the game,

we went to grab tacos with

Shimizu-san's friend Tinny.

It was a great time!

The next 3 days will be marathon training and networking events!

excerpt_en: June 23rd. Day 3 in America! Shimizu-san is creating GPS art, and I'm training for this year's Medoc Marathon in September. We have different goals, so our routes are different too. #OneYearSinceTaiwanCircumnavigation #NeverThoughtI'dBeRunning I
body_en:
June 23rd

Day 3 in America!

Shimizu-san is creating GPS art,

and I'm training for this year's Medoc Marathon in September.

We have different goals, so our routes are different too.

#OneYearSinceTaiwanCircumnavigation

#NeverThoughtIdBeRunning

In my case, I need to build a body that can run

at least 42 km, so I trained while visiting tourist spots.

I went to the pink Paul Smith and The Grove

on Melrose Avenue.

Honestly, it was way too hot in America and I got exhausted fast. Haha

Also, drinks are insanely expensive!! Haha

When you run, you get thirsty, and buying drinks at a convenience store or supermarket costs about 400 yen per bottle.

I ended up drinking 3 bottles today,

so I spent about 1,200 yen just on hydration.

I won't compromise when it comes to running healthily,

but I realized I should start running early in the morning

and finish by the morning.

#HeatDrainsEnergyAndMoney

Today I took lots of breaks

and alternated between running and walking over long distances.

About 22 km. It's my personal best,

but I felt like I could have done more.

Personally, by doing my Medoc Marathon training

in Los Angeles, I get to take in the cityscape at a leisurely pace -

maybe this is a new form of sightseeing? Haha

Once again, I really felt that

taking on new challenges is important!

In the evening, I met up with Tinny

and a Japanese guy named Kenji!

We had a toast at a Korean izakaya!!

Obviously,

the common language is "English."

Everyone except me speaks it fluently, naturally,

and I really felt that it's going to take time before I can naturally speak and listen in everyday situations.

It made me feel even more strongly

that I absolutely have to master English

within this year!

excerpt_en: June 24th. Running with GPS runner Shimizu-san! Today I joined in on the GPS art! I started out thinking 15 km would be good enough, but in the end, through running and walking, I covered about 37 km and
body_en:
June 24th

Running with GPS runner Shimizu-san!

Today I joined in on the GPS art!

I started out thinking 15 km would be good enough, but in the end, through running and walking, I covered about 37 km and drew Lincoln.

I smashed yesterday's 22 km

and set a new personal record! Haha

Apparently, among all the people who've joined Shimizu-san's GPS art,

this was the longest distance piece! Haha

#SettingARecordInLosAngeles

In my case, this was about building the stamina

to enjoy September's Medoc Marathon

and gaining long-distance experience,

but thanks to running with Shimizu-san,

I even got the bonus of "GPS Lincoln"!

Anyway,

the 2-day intensive training camp (self-proclaimed) is now complete!

My body is completely wrecked,

so I'm going to make sure to stretch properly!

In the late afternoon, the same group of 4 as the day before went to a famous observatory.

And in the evening, we went to a restaurant that Kenji recommended!

It was a really fulfilling time!!

excerpt_en: 2024/06/25. Last day of activities with Shimizu-san. In the morning, the two of us went to "The Broad," a free art museum. We checked out all sorts of art pieces. Since we hadn't secured tickets this time, we couldn't see Yayoi Kusama's art,
body_en:
2024/06/25

Last day of activities with Shimizu-san.

In the morning,

the two of us went to "The Broad," a free art museum.

We checked out all sorts of art pieces.

Since we hadn't secured tickets this time,

we couldn't see Yayoi Kusama's art,

so I want to try again next time.

Then we headed back to the accommodation once,

and the usual crew (4 of us) drove out.

We headed to the beaches on the west side.

There, two friends of Tinny's from Hong Kong joined us for lunch.

Oysters, paella, fried food, and IPA beer - the perfect combo.

Everything was so delicious that I ate a ton. Haha

After that, we dipped into the ocean a bit at the beach,

had a really fun time,

and then headed to Venice Beach and Santa Monica.

We soaked in the atmosphere.

It was already 9 PM.

Starting today, for the next 3 days,

I'd be staying at someone's house instead of a hostel,

so I headed to North Hollywood.

Actually, for this trip,

the killer was accommodation costs being so expensive, so I'd been using Couchsurfing in advance

as a new way to save money.

*Couchsurfing is a matching service between backpackers and hosts.

It was my first time trying it,

and I was a little nervous,

but having been an address hopper

without a home for two and a half years,

that nervousness quickly faded.

After a quick tour of the room,

I took a shower and relaxed in the living room.

Then my host, Igor, asked,

"Want some tea?"

Of course I said yes! And then he asked,

"Want to eat too?"

When I said "Just a little!",

he served me a massive plate of pasta.

Honestly, I'd eaten a huge lunch,

so I wasn't really hungry,

but since he'd gone to the trouble of making it, I decided to eat.

It turned into an unexpected

food challenge,

but I managed to finish it all.

Also, there was one

absolutely huge Doberman in the house.

I have a dog at my family's home too,

so it was totally fine,

but the power behind ball-play was on a whole different level -

even getting the ball away from the Doberman to play was a struggle! Haha

From the dog's perspective,

of course it would be wary of a complete stranger.

Still, petting calmed it down, so I was glad I'm used to being around dogs. Haha

Then, off to bed.

The bed was a king-size bed,

and my host and I shared the same bed.

For a second I thought, "Is this a sketchy situation??"

But nothing happened at all.

Since this is a matching service,

you really need to check the reviews or it could be dangerous,

so if you're going to use it the same way, please be careful!

So that's the deal -

for the next 3 days, I'll be staying at a house in North Hollywood!

excerpt_en: 2024/06/26. Day 6 of my stay in America. I'd been pretty busy with activities up to this point, and honestly, I hadn't been getting enough sleep. It was a rare day with nothing on the schedule, so I took the chance to sleep like a log,
body_en:
2024/06/26

Day 6 of my stay in America.

I'd been pretty busy with activities up to this point,

and honestly,

I hadn't been getting enough sleep.

It was a rare day with nothing on the schedule,

so I took the chance to sleep like a log

for about 10 hours.

After waking up and taking a shower,

I went to the living room

where my host Igor was,

and he said "Have some food,"

serving me yesterday's pasta.

Getting lunch provided for me too

is really something to be grateful for,

since eating out normally would cost at least $10, so I gratefully accepted.

After that, I went to a nearby library.

It had pretty good reviews,

and I was curious, so I checked it out.

I didn't read any books in particular,

but it had solid Wi-Fi,

so it was the perfect environment for getting work done.

I worked at the library for about 2 hours.

When I got back home, there were about 3 people I didn't know,

which surprised me.

They were speaking Russian,

so I had no idea what they were talking about,

and I just continued my work in the living room.

There are things about Couchsurfing

that I still don't quite understand,

like meals are basically your own responsibility,

but my host was constantly chatting with these unfamiliar people (we'd exchanged greetings) in Russian, so there was no way for me to jump in.

In the end, I went to bed without really talking to my host much.

Couchsurfing sure is tricky...
